# The prompt lifecycle: where extensions hook in {#sec:prompt-lifecycle}

The lifecycle is the map of extension hook points. Chapters 7-8 assume you can
read this path; this chapter traces the normal interactive lifecycle.
Before reading it, finish [TypeScript used by Pi](#sec:typescript) and
[Architecture](#sec:architecture). The later [Agent core](#sec:agent-core)
chapter explains the reusable loop in more detail; this chapter keeps the
focus on the end-to-end path.

It explains how event-driven transcript updates are requested, not terminal
renderer layout or paint internals; for those, follow the `InteractiveMode`
handlers into the interactive components directory,
`packages/coding-agent/src/modes/interactive/`, and then into
`components/` or `packages/tui`.

::: {.callout-note}
## Scope and sources
This chapter is source-grounded in the interactive mode and session files,
plus `packages/agent/src/agent.ts`, `packages/agent/src/agent-loop.ts`, and
the `packages/ai` dispatch layer. The exact call graph also has guards for
commands, compaction, retries, queued messages, and other modes; the numbered
path summarizes the current source, and its diagrams omit helper calls that do
not alter the data/control boundary being explained. You can skim these
details on a first read and treat the numbered stages as the map.
:::

## A small vocabulary for the trace

An **event** is a record saying that something happened. A **stream** is a
sequence of partial results delivered over time. A **turn** is one model
request and the tool work that follows it. A **settled** run is one with no
retry, compaction, or queued continuation left to perform. A Pi message is
composed of typed **content blocks** such as `text`, `thinking`, and `toolCall`,
rather than one plain string.

Use two prompts to keep the distinction concrete: `"What files changed?"`
usually creates one model turn, while `"Read the config and fix the failing
test"` can create several turns because the model may call `read`, then `bash`,
then answer. The path below explains what stays the same in both examples.

## The agent loop in one picture

**In plain terms:** Pi repeatedly gives a model three things—the assembled
system prompt, the conversation so far, and the active tool schemas. The model
then either answers or requests tools. Tool results become part of the
conversation for the next request. A run ends only when there are no tool calls
that require a model follow-up and no queued user messages.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-agent-loop-mental-model
%%| fig-cap: "The model-facing loop. Tool schemas and tool guidance have different roles: schemas are sent in Context; guidance helps construct the system prompt."
flowchart TD
  R["Resources:<br/>context files, skills, prompt options"] --> A["Assemble system prompt"]
  T["Active tool definitions:<br/>schemas, snippets, guidelines"] --> A
  T --> C
  M["Conversation messages"] --> C
  A --> C["Context:<br/>systemPrompt + messages + tools"]
  C --> P["Stream one assistant response"]
  P --> Q{"Tool calls?"}
  Q -->|"yes"| X["Validate, gate, execute,<br/>and append tool results"]
  X --> N{"All results<br/>terminate?"}
  N -->|"no"| C
  N -->|"yes"| U
  Q -->|"no"| U{"Queued message?"}
  U -->|"steering or follow-up"| C
  U -->|"none"| E["agent_end"]
  classDef assembly fill:#dbe7f5,stroke:#2f5b8f,color:#000000
  classDef loop fill:#fde2c4,stroke:#a05a00,color:#000000
  classDef end fill:#e9e9e9,stroke:#666666,color:#000000
  class A,C assembly
  class P,Q,X,N,U loop
  class E end
```

A **run** begins with `agent_start` and can contain several **turns**. A turn
is one provider response plus the tool batch it produces. `agent_end` means the
low-level loop stopped; `agent_settled` means that the coding-agent session has
also finished any retry, compaction-and-retry, or queued continuation work.

The loop has one important boundary: Pi's richer session messages are not sent
directly to a provider. Before every provider request, Pi can transform the
rich message list, then converts it to provider-compatible messages. The final
provider-facing `Context` contains exactly:

```ts
const context = {
  systemPrompt, // One assembled instruction string.
  messages, // Provider-compatible conversation messages.
  tools, // Active tool names, descriptions, and parameter schemas.
};
```

### How Pi builds the model context

This section answers one question: **what can the model see on its next
request?** The answer is not “everything Pi loaded or stored.” Every
model-visible value must become one of the three `Context` fields above. The
assembly has three timings:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-model-context-assembly
%%| fig-cap: "Model-context assembly. Resource discovery and input expansion happen before a run; messages are transformed and converted before every provider request."
flowchart LR
  subgraph START["Startup or reload"]
    R["Context files, skills,<br/>prompt options, extensions"]
    T["Built-in and extension<br/>tool definitions"]
  end
  subgraph INPUT["Submitted prompt"]
    I["Skill command or<br/>prompt-template expansion"]
    U["User and extension<br/>messages"]
  end
  R --> SP["Assemble<br/>system prompt"]
  T --> SP
  T --> AT["Select active tools"]
  I --> U
  U --> M["Rich AgentMessage[]"]
  SP --> C["Provider Context"]
  AT --> C
  M --> X["context event"] --> L["convertToLlm()"] --> C
  C --> P["Provider request"]
  classDef source fill:#dbe7f5,stroke:#2f5b8f,color:#000000
  classDef boundary fill:#fde2c4,stroke:#a05a00,color:#000000
  classDef output fill:#d7ecd7,stroke:#2e6b2e,color:#000000
  class R,T,I,U source
  class SP,AT,X,L boundary
  class C,P output
```

#### Model-visibility inventory

| Input | How Pi discovers or creates it | Model route | When it is visible |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pi default instructions, custom prompt, appended prompt, cwd | CLI options and `buildSystemPrompt()` | `systemPrompt` | Every request after assembly or refresh |
| `AGENTS.md` and other context files | Context-file discovery | Appended to `systemPrompt` | Every request |
| Available skills | Resource loader discovers configured/global/project skill files | A skill catalog—name, description, and location—inside `systemPrompt` | Every request when `read` is active, except skills marked `disable-model-invocation` |
| `/skill:name` invocation | Input expansion before a run | Full skill body plus typed arguments in the user message | The submitted message and later conversation history |
| Prompt template invocation | Input expansion before a run | Expanded text in the user message | The submitted message and later conversation history |
| Active built-in and extension tools | Tool registry and active-tool selection | Name, description, and parameter schema in `Context.tools`; optional guidance in `systemPrompt` | Every request after activation or refresh |
| Assistant text, tool calls, and tool results | Provider stream and tool execution | Converted assistant and `toolResult` messages | Later requests in the same active context |
| Extension custom messages | `before_agent_start`, `sendMessage`, or queued delivery | Converted message, if its type is model-visible | At its configured delivery point |
| Durable custom entries and renderers | `appendEntry()` and UI registration | No route by default | Never, unless an extension also sends a message |
| MCP connection/configuration | Pi has no built-in MCP subsystem | No route by default | Never, unless an extension exposes model-visible tools or messages |

#### System prompt: instructions and skill discovery

`AgentSession._rebuildSystemPrompt()` builds structured
`BuildSystemPromptOptions` and passes them to `buildSystemPrompt()`. In the
default-prompt path, it combines:

```text
Pi default base
+ --append-system-prompt text
+ discovered AGENTS.md/context files
+ available-skill catalog
+ active tools' prompt snippets and promptGuidelines
+ current working directory
= system-prompt string
```

A discovered skill is not copied wholesale into every request. Instead,
`formatSkillsForPrompt()` adds an `<available_skills>` entry containing its
name, description, and file location, plus instructions to use `read` when a
task matches. This is how the model discovers a skill. Skills with
`disable-model-invocation: true` are omitted from that catalog and can only be
invoked explicitly. The catalog is included only when `read` is active because
Pi's instruction tells the model to load the skill file with that tool.

An explicit `/skill:name arguments` follows a different path: before an agent
run starts, Pi reads the skill file and expands its full body, location, and the
user's arguments into the user message. Prompt templates likewise expand into
user-message text. These are message inputs, not system-prompt or tool-schema
inputs. [Customizing Pi](#sec:resources) documents discovery locations and
input expansion rules.

A custom `--system-prompt` replaces Pi's default base rather than extending it.
Pi still appends `--append-system-prompt`, context files, the current working
directory, and the available-skill catalog when `read` is active. The default
base, by contrast, includes the active tools' snippets and prompt guidelines.
This is why a replacement prompt must provide any tool-use instructions it
still needs.

#### Tools and MCP-backed capabilities

The current active tools are assembled beside the system-prompt string, not
inside it. One tool has three related but distinct model-facing
representations:

| Representation | Purpose | Where it goes |
|---|---|---|
| Tool definition | Name, description, TypeBox parameter schema, and `execute` function | Active `AgentTool` registry; its provider-compatible schema enters `Context.tools` |
| Prompt snippet and `promptGuidelines` | Prose that helps the model choose and use a tool | The default assembled system prompt |
| Tool result | Output, details, error state, and optional `terminate` hint | A `toolResult` conversation message after execution |

Pi initially activates `read`, `bash`, `edit`, and `write`; other built-in,
custom, and extension tools may be registered without being active. Activating a
tool is therefore a real model-policy change: it makes that tool schema
available in the next `Context` and rebuilds the system prompt with its
available prompt metadata. See [Built-in tools](#sec:tools) for the registry
and [Agent core](#sec:agent-core) for the generic contracts.

Pi does not provide MCP integration as a built-in subsystem. An MCP extension
or another bridge can connect to an MCP server, but that connection is
model-invisible by itself. The model sees an MCP-backed capability only when
the integration registers and activates a Pi tool, or deliberately injects a
model-visible message. Connection state, credentials, cached data, and UI-only
rendering remain local unless the extension explicitly converts them into one
of those `Context` fields.

### The continuation rule in simplified code

The actual implementation emits events, supports parallel tools, and refreshes
next-turn state. Its control flow can be read as this deliberately simplified
version:

```ts
while (true) {
  const response = await streamModel(context);
  if (response.stopReason === "error" || response.stopReason === "aborted") break;

  const calls = response.toolCalls;
  const results = await validateGateAndExecute(calls);
  context.messages.push(response, ...results);

  if (calls.length > 0 && !results.every((result) => result.terminate)) continue;

  const steering = takeSteeringMessages();
  if (steering.length > 0) {
    context.messages.push(...steering);
    continue;
  }

  const followUp = takeFollowUpMessages();
  if (followUp.length > 0) {
    context.messages.push(...followUp);
    continue;
  }

  break;
}
```

This sketch preserves the rules that matter most: a failed tool becomes an
error result the model can inspect, rather than ending the run; one terminating
tool does not stop a batch unless **every** finalized result terminates; and
steering is checked before follow-up. The real loop also calls
`prepareNextTurn` after each turn. Coding-agent uses that refresh point so a
later request can see changed active tools, a rebuilt system prompt, model, or
thinking level without changing the request already in progress. An embedding
can also supply `shouldStopAfterTurn`; when it returns true, the generic loop
ends before it polls steering or follow-up queues. Coding-agent's normal
continuation behavior instead uses the queue rules shown above and its
session-level recovery policy.

## The complete path

**In plain terms:** your text travels from the editor, through interception and
context construction, to the provider, and back through streaming events, tool
execution, and persistence. The map below is the whole chapter in one picture;
each stage section then zooms in. As a running example, picture the prompt
`"Read the config and fix the failing test"`: it produces several turns because
the model calls `read`, then `bash`, then answers.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-prompt-lifecycle-overview
%%| fig-cap: "The complete interactive prompt path. Prompt and tool assembly precede the repeated provider/tool cycle; persistence and session settlement happen after the low-level loop ends."
flowchart TD
  S1["1-3. Input, interception,<br/>and run construction"]:::input
  S4["4. Assemble prompt,<br/>messages, and active tools"]:::assembly
  S56["5-6. Provider Context<br/>and streaming response"]:::turn
  S7["7. Validate and execute tools"]:::turn
  S8{"8. Continue this run?"}
  S9["9-10. Persistence,<br/>recovery, and settlement"]:::settle
  S1 --> S4 --> S56 --> S7 --> S8
  S8 -->|"yes"| S4
  S8 -->|"no"| S9
  classDef input fill:#dbe7f5,stroke:#2f5b8f,color:#000000
  classDef assembly fill:#d7ecd7,stroke:#2e6b2e,color:#000000
  classDef turn fill:#fde2c4,stroke:#a05a00,color:#000000
  classDef settle fill:#e9e9e9,stroke:#666666,color:#000000
```

## Stage 1: Terminal input enters the TUI

**In plain terms:** pressing Enter hands your text to the session; pressing it
while the model is still working queues the text for a later turn instead of
interrupting.

`InteractiveMode` owns the coding-agent's interactive screen. Its editor
submission handler recognizes built-in slash commands and `!`/`!!` user-shell
commands before normal agent prompting. A normal idle submission is queued or
resolved through `getUserInput()`. The `run()` loop awaits that method, then
calls `await this.session.prompt(userInput)`.

When Pi is already streaming, regular Enter calls `session.prompt(text,
{ streamingBehavior: "steer" })`; Alt+Enter uses `"followUp"` (the editor keys
are covered in [Using Pi day to day](#sec:using-pi)). **Steering** waits
for the current assistant response and its tool work to finish, then injects
the message before the next provider request. A **follow-up** is held until
there are no more tool calls or steering messages and the run would otherwise
stop, then starts another turn. Neither starts a second concurrent agent run;
each call queues a message at that exact loop boundary. Esc takes the opposite
path: it aborts the active run rather than queuing input — Stage 10 covers
that. **Compaction** summarizes older context into a durable entry so the
model's context window is not exceeded; [Sessions and context](#sec:sessions)
covers it in detail. During compaction, non-command input is held by
interactive mode until the compaction flow completes.

The initial prompt supplied by command-line arguments travels through the same
`session.prompt()` API. Print and [RPC](#sec:glossary) modes do not instantiate
`InteractiveMode`, but they converge on `AgentSession` rather than
reimplementing the provider path.

**Why an extension author cares:** steering and follow-up are the same queue
modes you select with `sendUserMessage(text, { deliverAs: "steer" | "followUp" })`
— input you inject rides the boundaries described here.

## Stage 2: Commands, keybindings, and input interception

**In plain terms:** before your text becomes a prompt, Pi gives two command
systems and every extension a chance to claim or rewrite it.

There are two distinct command systems:

- Built-in interactive commands such as `/model`, `/settings`, `/new`, and
  `/compact` are handled in `InteractiveMode` before a normal prompt reaches
  the session.
- Extension commands registered with `pi.registerCommand()` are detected first
  inside `AgentSession.prompt()`. They execute immediately, including while an
  agent is streaming, and do not enter the model context unless their handler
  explicitly sends a message.

For ordinary text, `AgentSession.prompt()` asks the extension runner to emit
an `input` event. The event sees raw text after extension-command lookup and
before skill/template expansion. An extension can continue, transform, or
handle it. If it continues, Pi expands `/skill:name ...`; skills are reusable
model instructions and prompt templates are parameterized prompt snippets. Pi
then continues with the expanded text.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-input-routing
%%| fig-cap: "Stage 2 input routing. Built-in and extension commands are claimed before the input event fires; only unclaimed text reaches skill and template expansion and becomes a prompt."
%%{init: {"flowchart": {"nodeSpacing": 12, "rankSpacing": 10}, "themeVariables": {"fontSize": "11px"}}}%%
flowchart TD
  B{"Built-in?"}
  B -->|yes| BH["InteractiveMode<br/>handles it"]:::done
  B -->|no| X{"Extension?"}
  X -->|yes| XH["Handler runs;<br/>event skipped"]:::done
  X -->|no| I["Input event:<br/>continue, transform,<br/>or handle"]:::hook
  I -->|continue| E["Expand skill/template;<br/>becomes prompt"]:::done
  classDef hook fill:#fde2c4,stroke:#a05a00,color:#000000
  classDef done fill:#e9e9e9,stroke:#666666,color:#000000
```

**Hook selection comes first:** built-in commands have already been handled by
`InteractiveMode`, and a matching registered extension command has already
returned before `input` is emitted. Therefore `input` is the interception point
for ordinary text and unclaimed slash text. An extension that wants to
intercept a skill invocation must use `input`; one that wants a durable custom
command should use `registerCommand`, not parse slash text manually.

A minimal extension that uses both interception points (save it, then load
with `pi -e ./input-routing.ts` or copy it to `~/.pi/agent/extensions/` and
restart or `/reload`):

```typescript
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";

export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
  // A durable custom command: /my-cmd runs immediately, even mid-stream.
  pi.registerCommand("my-cmd", {
    description: "Show a notification",
    handler: async (_args, ctx) => ctx.ui.notify("ran /my-cmd", "info"),
  });
  // Ordinary text: rewrite it before skill/template expansion.
  pi.on("input", async (event) => {
    if (event.text.startsWith("?quick "))
      return {
        action: "transform",
        text: `Respond briefly: ${event.text.slice(7)}`,
      };
  });
}
```

**Why an extension author cares:** this stage decides which interception point
is yours — `registerCommand()` for a new slash command, the `input` event for
transforming or handling ordinary text.

## Stage 3: Agent-session orchestration constructs a turn

**In plain terms:** the session validates that a run can start, gathers your
message plus any buffered extension messages, notifies extensions that a run is
beginning, and hands everything to the generic agent loop.

`AgentMessage` is the type the session passes around: it is either a
provider-ready LLM `Message` or a coding-agent-specific custom message type.
That union lets the session keep rich application messages (custom UI entries,
tool details) alongside the plain messages the model will eventually see, even
when the later conversion step maps them to a different LLM representation.

For a fresh run, `AgentSession.prompt()` validates the selected model and
credentials, checks whether compaction is needed, and creates a user message.
It then appends pending `nextTurn` messages: custom messages buffered by
`pi.sendMessage(..., { deliverAs: "nextTurn" })` (the extension API; it calls
`AgentSession.sendCustomMessage()` internally). Buffering for `nextTurn` lets
an extension attach context to the *next* fresh user prompt — for example,
notes a command just computed — instead of injecting into a run that is
already active.

The three delivery modes that appear in this chapter:

| Delivery mode | Buffered when | Delivered when | Selected with |
|---|---|---|---|
| `steer` | While a run is streaming | After the current assistant response and its tool work finish, before the next provider request | Enter while streaming; `sendUserMessage(text, { deliverAs: "steer" })` |
| `followUp` | While a run is streaming | Only when the run has no more tool calls or steering messages and would otherwise stop | Alt+Enter while streaming; `{ deliverAs: "followUp" }` |
| `nextTurn` | Any time, by an extension | Appended to the next fresh user prompt; never interrupts or triggers a run | `pi.sendMessage(msg, { deliverAs: "nextTurn" })` |

Next the session emits `before_agent_start`, whose handlers can add custom
messages and chain a system-prompt override, then calls
`_runAgentPrompt(messages)`. A minimal handler:

```typescript
pi.on("before_agent_start", async (event) => {
  return {
    // Extra message the model will see (persisted in the session):
    message: { customType: "my-ext", content: "Extra context", display: true },
    // Chained system-prompt override for this turn:
    systemPrompt: event.systemPrompt + "\n\nPrefer small, reversible edits.",
  };
});
```

`_runAgentPrompt()` calls `Agent.prompt()`; the reusable Agent API is detailed
in [Agent core](#sec:agent-core). It can later call `Agent.continue()`
for automatic retry, automatic compaction retry, or work queued by an
`agent_end` handler. On settlement it clears the per-run prompt override,
flushes pending user-bash messages, and emits `agent_settled`.

The `Agent` owns the abort controller, streaming state, accumulated messages,
active tools, and queues. `Agent.prompt()` snapshots its state and starts
`runAgentLoop()`.

**Why an extension author cares:** `before_agent_start` is your last chance to
add messages or override the system prompt before the loop takes over;
`agent_settled` is the signal that all follow-up work is done.

## Stage 4: Context and system prompt cross three construction boundaries

**In plain terms:** Pi builds a system prompt for the model, lets extensions
adjust the message list, and then converts the result into the exact format the
provider API expects. [The agent loop in one picture](#the-agent-loop-in-one-picture)
shows how this assembly relates to tool schemas, tool guidance, and continuation;
this stage identifies the extension and provider boundaries in that same path.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-context-boundaries
%%| fig-cap: "Stage 4's three construction boundaries. The extension context event (amber) sits between base system-prompt assembly and provider conversion; the output is a Context with exactly systemPrompt, messages, and tools."
flowchart TD
  SP["base system prompt<br/>_rebuildSystemPrompt()"] --> TC["transformContext<br/>extension context event"]:::hook
  TC --> CL["convertToLlm()"]
  CL --> CTX["Context:<br/>systemPrompt + messages + tools"]:::out
  classDef hook fill:#fde2c4,stroke:#a05a00,color:#000000
  classDef out fill:#d7ecd7,stroke:#2e6b2e,color:#000000
```

| Boundary | Owner in the runtime | What it produces |
|----------------|------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Base system-prompt assembly | `AgentSession._rebuildSystemPrompt()` and `buildSystemPrompt()` | System-prompt text from cwd, context files, skills, prompts, and active-tool guidance |
| Extension context transform | `Agent`'s `transformContext` callback, wired to the extension `context` event | An adjusted `AgentMessage[]` list before provider conversion |
| LLM/provider conversion | `convertToLlm()` followed by `streamFn`/`streamSimple()` | A provider-compatible `Context`, then the provider's assistant event stream |

One scoping note for everything below: the model was already chosen earlier,
during `createAgentSession()` in `core/sdk.ts` (session restore, configured
default, or `findInitialModel()`), and `Agent.prompt()` snapshots that model
together with the system prompt, tools, and transcript when a run starts.
These three boundaries transform context *for* that already-chosen model; they
never select one. (The session can still change `Agent.state.model` later, for
example through `/model`.)

First, the coding agent builds a base system prompt in
`AgentSession._rebuildSystemPrompt()`. That method combines the working
directory, loaded `AGENTS.md`/context files, loaded skills, custom and appended
system prompts, and selected tools plus their snippets and guidelines.
`buildSystemPrompt()` in `core/system-prompt.ts` converts the structured data to
text. Changing active tools rebuilds the base prompt, so tool prompt metadata
can affect the next model turn.

Immediately before *each* model request, `streamAssistantResponse()` in
`agent-loop.ts` crosses the other two boundaries in sequence. The distinction
that matters: `transformContext` works on Pi's rich `AgentMessage[]` list (the
union from Stage 3, including custom message types), while `convertToLlm()`
converts that list into the plain, provider-compatible `Message[]` the
provider API accepts. Coding-agent supplies both callbacks when creating
`Agent` in `core/sdk.ts`:

- `transformContext` dispatches the extension `context` event. A handler
  receives a deep copy of the message list and returns a replacement, so it
  can reshape what the model is about to see per request — for example,
  pruning stale scratchpad messages:

  ```typescript
  pi.on("context", async (event) => {
    // event.messages: deep copy of the AgentMessage[] list; safe to modify.
    return { messages: event.messages.filter((m) => !isStaleScratchpad(m)) };
  });
  ```

- `convertToLlm` filters and normalizes coding-agent-specific message types.
  When `blockImages` (the `images.blockImages` setting in `settings.json`) is
  enabled, this conversion-time step replaces image content in converted user
  and tool-result messages with a text placeholder, immediately before
  provider dispatch. It is dynamic, so a setting change affects subsequent
  conversions.

The resulting [`Context`](#sec:glossary) has exactly `systemPrompt`,
`messages`, and `tools`. That shape lets the session persist rich UI-only
entries while only a selected representation reaches the LLM.

**Why an extension author cares:** the `context` event is where you reshape the
message list the model is about to see — per request, not just at run start.

## Stage 5: Provider dispatch for the already-chosen model

**In plain terms:** the loop hands the normalized context and the chosen model
to a small dispatch function, which adds credentials and headers and picks the
provider adapter that speaks the vendor's wire protocol.

::: {.callout-note}
## Skippable on first read
This stage dives into credential and header resolution. The one thing to
remember on a first pass is where the three provider hooks fire; come back for
the wiring details when you need them.
:::

This stage charts the coding-agent stream path from the snapshotted `Model` to a
provider adapter. Model *catalog* selection and runtime changes belong to
session creation and `/model` (see Stage 4's opening and [Models, providers,
and authentication](#sec:models)); they are not re-decided here.

Coding-agent installs a custom `streamFn` on `Agent` in `core/sdk.ts`:
`streamFn` is the function the agent loop calls for every model request, and it
defaults to `streamSimple()` from the [compat layer](#sec:glossary) — the
compatibility API in `packages/ai/src/compat.ts` that resolves a provider
implementation from `model.api` and streams through it. For each assistant
request the path is:

1. `streamAssistantResponse()` (agent-loop) calls `streamFn(model, context,
   options)` with the snapshotted model and the Stage 4 `Context`.
2. The `streamFn` first resolves auth: it looks up credentials with
   `modelRegistry.getApiKeyAndHeaders(model)` and merges provider-attribution
   and request headers (`mergeProviderAttributionHeaders`). Then it emits the
   extension `before_provider_headers` event so handlers can mutate the
   assembled headers before anything is sent:

   ```typescript
   pi.on("before_provider_headers", (event, ctx) => {
     // Add or override a header (mutate event.headers in place):
     event.headers["x-session-id"] = ctx.sessionManager.getSessionId();
     // Delete a header by setting it to null:
     event.headers["X-OpenRouter-Title"] = null;
   });
   ```

3. With headers ready, `streamFn` calls `streamSimple(model, context, options)`
   from `packages/ai/src/compat.ts`, passing `apiKey`, merged headers,
   timeouts, and retries. Agent core also forwards `onPayload` / `onResponse`
   from the `Agent` instance into those options (wired in `createLoopConfig()`).
4. `compat.streamSimple` picks the adapter: it either delegates to built-in
   model support (`compatModels.streamSimple`) when the model matches a
   registered builtin API instance, or looks up the registered API provider by
   `model.api` and calls that provider's `streamSimple`.
5. The adapter serializes the normalized context and returns a
   provider-independent assistant event stream. During that path:
   - `onPayload` becomes extension `before_provider_request` and may replace
     the provider-specific payload after serialization.
   - `onResponse` becomes extension `after_provider_response` and observes HTTP
     status/headers before body consumption when the adapter exposes them.

`ModelRegistry` still owns the catalog, configured provider overrides,
credential lookup, OAuth state, and header sources used by step 2; its role in
this stage is auth/header resolution for the chosen model, not model selection.

The sequence below summarizes stages 3-6 for one turn of the running example —
the first model request after `"Read the config and fix the failing test"`:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-turn-sequence
%%| fig-cap: "One turn across stages 3-6: turn construction, context conversion, provider dispatch, and the event stream back to the session. The turn begins when the editor calls AgentSession.prompt(text)."
sequenceDiagram
  participant S as AgentSession
  participant X as Extensions
  participant L as Agent loop
  S->>X: before_agent_start
  S->>L: prompt(messages)
  L->>X: context event, convertToLlm
  L->>L: streamSimple to provider
  L-->>S: message events
  L->>X: tool_call gate
  L->>L: execute tools
  L-->>S: toolResult
```

Scope boundary for this chapter: the numbered path stops at adapter
`streamSimple` and the three provider-extension insertion points. Vendor wire
formats, transport negotiation, custom provider registration, and auth-storage
details live in [Models, providers, and authentication](#sec:models) and the
provider-hook reference in
`packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md` (`before_provider_headers`,
`before_provider_request`, `after_provider_response`). Those headers/payload
hooks are not substitutes for a custom provider; use `pi.registerProvider()`
when you must declare a model catalog, endpoint, auth, or streaming
implementation.

**Why an extension author cares:** the header/payload hooks
(`before_provider_headers`, `before_provider_request`,
`after_provider_response`) fire inside this stage, in the order shown in the
turn diagram above.

## Stage 6: Streaming response becomes agent events and TUI updates

**In plain terms:** the provider's partial replies are consumed chunk by chunk
and republished as Pi's own events; the transcript you see typing out is those
events being rendered.

`streamAssistantResponse()` consumes the `AssistantMessageEventStream` with
`for await`: this is the async-iteration boundary where provider deltas —
the small partial chunks a provider emits, such as a few tokens of text at a
time — become agent events. The [Agent core](#sec:agent-core) chapter shows
the same loop from the reusable runtime's point of view. It creates a partial
assistant message on `start`, replaces that partial message as text/thinking/
tool-call deltas arrive, and emits `message_start`, `message_update`, and
`message_end` events.

`Agent.processEvents()` reduces those events into state and then awaits all
subscribers. `AgentSession` bridges generic events to persistence, extensions,
and application behavior. `InteractiveMode` updates the visible transcript,
current streaming row, tool rows, footer, and render requests. The TUI package
performs the actual terminal rendering.

The extension lifecycle nests inside the run like this:

1. One run: `agent_start` ... `agent_end`.
2. Each turn inside the run: `turn_start` ... `turn_end`.
3. Each message inside a turn: `message_start`, then many `message_update`
   events as deltas arrive, then `message_end`.

For canonical detail, use [Agent core](#sec:agent-core) for loop state and
queue semantics, [Built-in tools](#sec:tools) for tool contracts,
[Extension event reference](#sec:events) for event payloads, and [Failure and
recovery](#sec:failure) for recovery behavior. This chapter keeps only the
end-to-end trace.

**Why an extension author cares:** `message_start`/`message_update`/
`message_end` and the turn events are how you observe model output live — for
example to stream progress into custom UI.

## Stage 7: Tool calls are parsed, validated, gated, and executed

**In plain terms:** when the model asks to run tools, Pi checks each request
against the tool's schema, lets extensions veto or modify it, executes it, and
appends the result where the model can see it. In the running example, this is
where the model's `read` call on the config file is checked and executed.

After a final assistant message, `agent-loop.ts` filters content blocks whose
`type` is `"toolCall"`. A `"length"` stop means the token limit cut off the
assistant output, so any tool call may contain truncated JSON arguments — for
example `{"path": "src/comp` — even if its parsed block appears complete. The
loop therefore fails every tool call in that message rather than risk side
effects from incomplete arguments.

For each normal tool call, the loop's short pipeline is:

1. Emit `tool_execution_start`, look up the active tool, and prepare and
   validate its arguments against the TypeBox schema (a runtime validation
   schema; see [TypeBox](#sec:glossary)).
2. Run the `beforeToolCall` gate (coding-agent's `tool_call` event), then call
   `tool.execute(id, args, signal, onUpdate)`.
3. Turn `onUpdate` values into update events, apply the `afterToolCall`
   (`tool_result`) patch, emit `tool_execution_end`, and append a `toolResult`
   message.

The `tool_call` gate is where an extension can veto or rewrite a call before
anything executes. This example rejects dangerous `bash` commands:

```typescript
pi.on("tool_call", async (event) => {
  if (event.toolName === "bash" && /\brm\s+-rf\b/.test(String(event.input.command))) {
    // The model receives this reason as an error toolResult and can recover.
    return { block: true, reason: "Refusing to run rm -rf" };
  }
  // Mutating event.input in place would instead rewrite the arguments.
});
```

See [Built-in tools](#sec:tools) for the contract and [Extension event
reference](#sec:events) for payloads and hook ordering.

Tool execution defaults to parallel. Agent core sets `toolExecution` to
`"parallel"` (`options.toolExecution ?? "parallel"` in
`packages/agent/src/agent.ts`; see [Agent core](#sec:agent-core) for the
ordering contract), and coding-agent's `new Agent({...})` in `core/sdk.ts`
does not override it. A caller of agent core can select sequential execution,
and a tool declaring `executionMode: "sequential"` makes its containing batch
sequential.

In parallel mode the ordering guarantees are: [preflight](#sec:glossary) (the
`beforeToolCall` / `tool_call` gate each call passes through before any tool
runs) happens in assistant source order; successful tool executions may run
concurrently; and final `toolResult` messages are emitted in assistant source
order.

::: {.callout-warning}
Start/update/end event ordering differs in parallel mode: `tool_execution_end`
fires in tool completion order, so a `tool_call` or `tool_result` handler is
not guaranteed to see sibling tool results from the same assistant message.
See the extension documentation before using sibling tool results as state.
:::

Built-in tools are supplied by coding-agent. They are ordinary tools at this
boundary, which is why an extension can add a tool or override a built-in one.
Custom filesystem-mutating tools must participate in
`withFileMutationQueue()` — a per-path async queue from
`packages/coding-agent/src/core/tools/file-mutation-queue.ts` that serializes
writes to the same file — to avoid lost writes when sibling tools run in
parallel; the [Built-in tools](#sec:tools) chapter has the runnable queue
skeleton.

**Why an extension author cares:** the `tool_call` event is your safety gate
(veto or rewrite arguments before execution) and `tool_result` lets you patch
what the model sees afterwards.

## Stage 8: Results create the next agent action

**In plain terms:** tool results go back to the model; final text ends the turn
unless someone — you, via steering or follow-up — has queued more to say. In
the running example, the `read` result returns here and starts the turn where
the model calls `bash` to run the test; when the model finally answers with
plain text, this is where the run decides to stop.

Each final tool result is appended to the in-memory agent context and becomes
the model-facing material for the next turn. This includes schema failures,
blocked calls, and thrown tool errors: they become error `toolResult` messages
so the model can correct or explain the failure. If an assistant response
contains tool calls and their batch is not explicitly terminating, the loop
starts another assistant response. If the model instead returns ordinary final
text, the loop checks steering messages first and follow-up messages when it
would otherwise stop:

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-next-action
%%| fig-cap: "After a toolResult is appended, the loop decides what happens next. Queued steering beats follow-up; an empty loop emits agent_end."
flowchart TD
  Q1{"tool calls and<br/>not terminating?"}
  Q1 -->|"yes"| NT["next model turn"]:::cont
  Q1 -->|"no"| Q2{"steering queued?"}
  Q2 -->|"yes"| NT
  Q2 -->|"no"| Q3{"follow-up queued?"}
  Q3 -->|"yes"| NT
  Q3 -->|"no"| END["agent_end"]:::term
  classDef cont fill:#d7ecd7,stroke:#2e6b2e,color:#000000
  classDef term fill:#e9e9e9,stroke:#666666,color:#000000
```

A tool can return `terminate: true` to hint that the next model call should be
skipped, but the batch terminates only when every finalized tool result sets
that flag. A tool signals failure by throwing; merely returning a field named
`isError` does not mark it failed.

**Why an extension author cares:** messages you queued with
`deliverAs: "steer"` or `"followUp"` are drained here, in that priority order.

## Stage 9: Persistence and transcript updates settle the turn

**In plain terms:** everything that happened is appended to the durable session
file and, in parallel, to the on-screen transcript; custom UI renderers only
change the screen, never the model loop.

As message events complete, `AgentSession` records them through its
`SessionManager`. The session is a JSONL tree, so the persisted form can later
rebuild a branch and compact context. Model and thinking-level changes are also
session [entries](#sec:glossary). Extension state added with `appendEntry()`
is persisted as a custom entry — [durable](#sec:glossary) across restarts and
reloads — but remains outside LLM context.

`InteractiveMode.handleEvent()` responds to the same event stream. Assistant
updates request transcript updates for the streaming message; tool lifecycle
events manage pending tool rows; finalized messages become permanent transcript
components. Renderer registration lets an extension replace the display of its
custom messages, entries, or tool calls/results without changing how the model
loop works.

::: {.callout-note}
## Scope note
This chapter stops at the event-to-component boundary: how events request
transcript updates, not how the terminal paints them. Terminal rendering
internals live in `packages/tui` and [Terminal UI](#sec:tui).
:::

**Why an extension author cares:** `appendEntry()` persists your state without
polluting model context, and renderer registration controls how your custom
messages and entries look on screen.

## Stage 10: Errors, cancellation, retries, compaction, and shutdown

**In plain terms:** a run can fail, be cancelled, retry, compact, and finally
settle; `agent_end` only means the loop stopped, while `agent_settled` means
nothing automatic is left to do.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-run-lifecycle
%%| fig-cap: "Run lifecycle. Retries and compaction resume the run; agent_end fires when the loop stops, agent_settled when no automatic continuation remains."
stateDiagram-v2
  running: running
  retrying: retrying
  compacting: compacting
  ended: agent_end<br/>loop stopped
  settled: agent_settled<br/>no retry or queue left
  aborted: aborted
  running --> retrying: provider error
  running --> compacting: context too large
  retrying --> running
  compacting --> running
  running --> ended
  ended --> settled
  running --> aborted: Esc / abort signal
```

The low-level loop deliberately separates provider failures from tool
failures:

| Failure type | What the model sees | Recovery path |
|---|---|---|
| Provider error or abort | An assistant message with `stopReason` `"error"` or `"aborted"` | The low-level run ends; `AgentSession` may retry automatically |
| Tool schema error, blocked call, or thrown execution | An error `toolResult` message | The model sees the failure and can recover in a later turn |

Esc aborts the active `AbortController`; provider, tool, and extension work
must honor the signal. For the complete recovery matrix, see [Failure and
recovery](#sec:failure).

Above this boundary, `AgentSession` may retry, compact older context, rebuild
it, and continue; [Sessions and context](#sec:sessions) explains compaction.
Session replacement or process exit emits `session_shutdown`, where
session-bound resources should be cleaned up. `agent_end` means the current
low-level loop stopped. `agent_settled` is later: Pi has no automatic retry,
compaction retry, or queued continuation left.

One automatic retry makes the difference concrete: the provider errors, so
`agent_end` fires for the first low-level run; `AgentSession` retries and the
second run completes, so `agent_end` fires again; only then does
`agent_settled` fire — twice for `agent_end`, once for `agent_settled`. Status
integrations and cleanup that require true idleness should use `agent_settled`
or `ctx.waitForIdle()` in a command context.

**Why an extension author cares:** honor the abort signal in your own async
work, clean up session-bound resources on `session_shutdown`, and use
`agent_settled` (not `agent_end`) when you need true idleness.

## Trace this yourself

All source paths in this chapter are relative to the repository root. For a
live trace, start at these symbols in order:

1. `InteractiveMode.run()` and `getUserInput()` in\
   `packages/coding-agent/src/modes/interactive/interactive-mode.ts`.
2. `AgentSession.prompt()` and `_runAgentPrompt()` in\
   `packages/coding-agent/src/core/agent-session.ts`.
3. `Agent.prompt()`, `createLoopConfig()`, and `processEvents()` in
   `packages/agent/src/agent.ts`.
4. `runAgentLoop()`, `streamAssistantResponse()`, and `executeToolCalls()` in\
   `packages/agent/src/agent-loop.ts`.
5. `streamSimple()` in `packages/ai/src/compat.ts`.
6. The `streamSimple` function in the selected provider API adapter.

Use the extension event timeline in `packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md`
as a second view of the same lifecycle. It documents what extension authors
can rely on; the source symbols above show which layer makes each guarantee.

## Stage-to-surface bridge

| Stage | Hook or API available there |
|---|---|
| Input | `sendUserMessage`, `deliverAs: "steer"` or `"followUp"` |
| Interception | `registerCommand`, `input` event |
| Turn construction | `before_agent_start`, `sendMessage`, `triggerTurn` |
| Context boundaries | `context` event, `getSystemPromptOptions()` |
| Provider dispatch | `before_provider_headers`, `before_provider_request`, `after_provider_response`, `registerProvider` |
| Streaming | `message_start`/`update`/`end`, `turn_start`/`end`, custom UI |
| Tools | `tool_call`, `tool_result`, `registerTool` |
| Next action | steering and follow-up queues |
| Persistence | `appendEntry`, message and entry renderers |
| Settlement | `agent_end`, `agent_settled`, `session_shutdown`, `waitForIdle` |

This table is the bridge into the extension recipes. Use the [extension event
reference](#sec:events) for exact payloads and return values.

## Try it

1. Write an extension that logs every tool gate:
   `pi.on("tool_call", async (event) => console.log(event.toolName, event.input))`.
   Load it with `pi -e ./log-tools.ts`, ask Pi to read a file, and answer:
   which stage does `tool_call` fire in, and which events fire immediately
   before and after it? (Check your answer against the timeline in
   `packages/coding-agent/docs/extensions.md`.)
2. Using the delivery-mode table in Stage 3, predict what happens when you
   type a message and press Enter while Pi is mid-stream, then do it and
   confirm the message is injected before the next provider request, not
   appended at the end.
3. Load the Stage 7 `tool_call` block example, ask Pi to remove a scratch
   directory with `rm -rf`, and confirm two things: the command never runs,
   and the model receives your `reason` as an error `toolResult` it can
   respond to.
